inline_review:
commit 7e17cbe1dc95a0cd1a1410be38ab6964f78fb15c
Author: Josh Law <objecting@objecting.org>

mm/damon/core: validate goal nid before accessing node data

This commit adds bounds checking and node_online() validation for the
goal->nid in the DAMON quota metrics. It prevents out-of-bounds accesses
to NODE_DATA() when an invalid or offline NUMA node is provided by userspace.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index db6c67e52d2b..81ce27baa1a1 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2245,6 +2245,10 @@ static __kernel_ulong_t damos_get_node_mem_bp(
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  	__kernel_ulong_t numerator;
>  
> +	if (goal->nid < 0 || goal->nid >= MAX_NUMNODES ||
> +	    !node_online(goal->nid))
> +		return 0;
> +
>  	si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid);
>  	if (goal->metric == DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_USED_BP)
>  		numerator = i.totalram - i.freeram;
>  	else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */
>  		numerator = i.freeram;
>  	return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);

Could this cause a division by zero if the node is online but memoryless?

For CPU-only compute nodes or nodes where all memory has been hot-removed,
node_online(goal->nid) will evaluate to true. When si_meminfo_node() is
called for these memoryless nodes, it populates the sysinfo struct with
i.totalram = 0.

The mult_frac() macro unconditionally performs integer division and modulo
by the third argument, which could trigger a kernel panic when dividing by
zero.

Would it be safer to check if i.totalram is zero before returning, or use
node_state(goal->nid, N_MEMORY) instead of node_online()?

[ ... ]

> @@ -2261,6 +2265,10 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
>  	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  
> +	if (goal->nid < 0 || goal->nid >= MAX_NUMNODES ||
> +	    !node_online(goal->nid))
> +		return 0;
> +
>  	memcg = mem_cgroup_get_from_id(goal->memcg_id);

The same division by zero risk exists here when mult_frac() is called at the
end of this function.
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Division by zero kernel panic when userspace provides an online memoryless node ID.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"This problem wasn't introduced by this patch, but the added checks fail to prevent a critical flaw that can lead to a Denial of Service. The patch adds a `!node_online(goal->nid)` check to prevent out-of-bounds accesses. However, a NUMA node can be online but completely memoryless (e.g., CPU-only compute nodes, or nodes where all memory has been hot-removed). For such memoryless nodes, `node_online(goal->nid)` evaluates to true. 

When `si_meminfo_node(&i, goal->nid)` is called for a memoryless node, it populates the `sysinfo` struct with `i.totalram = 0`. Subsequently, the code calls `mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram)`. The `mult_frac(x, n, d)` macro unconditionally performs integer division and modulo by the third argument (`d`). Passing `0` as the denominator triggers a hardware divide-by-zero exception (Kernel Oops/Panic).

Since `goal->nid` is configured from userspace via the DAMON sysfs interface, this allows a user with DAMON access to easily crash the kernel. To fix this, an explicit check like `if (!i.totalram) return 0;` should be added before calling `mult_frac()`, or `node_state(goal->nid, N_MEMORY)` should be used instead of `node_online()`."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 7e17cbe1dc95a0cd1a1410be38ab6964f78fb15c

mm/damon/core: validate goal nid before accessing node data

>  	else	/* DAMOS_QUOTA_NODE_MEM_FREE_BP */
>  		numerator = i.freeram;
>  	return mult_frac(numerator, 10000, i.totalram);

For CPU-only compute nodes or nodes where all memory has been hot-removed,

[ ... ]

> @@ -2261,6 +2265,10 @@ static unsigned long damos_get_node_memcg_used_bp(
>  	unsigned long used_pages, numerator;
>  	struct sysinfo i;
>  
> +	if (goal->nid < 0 || goal->nid >= MAX_NUMNODES ||

> +		return 0;

The same division by zero risk exists here when mult_frac() is called at the
